title: product-cli — Product Framework Domain & Event Model

contexts.label: The Model (What)
contexts.purpose: §3 — The What. The structural domain model (bounded contexts, entities, value objects, relations, invariants, context mappings) and the event/behaviour model (events, commands, read models, wireframe steps, flows) that together describe what a product is and does.

contexts.label: The Why
contexts.purpose: §4.1 — The reasoning layer of the How: foundational top decisions, the principles they license, and the patterns that realise those principles. Each must earn its place.

contexts.label: Contracts
contexts.purpose: §4.2–4.4 — The binding How: application contracts (invariant code-shaping decisions), infrastructure contracts (concrete runtime choices), the repository layout model, and interface contracts generated from the model.

contexts.label: Realisation
contexts.purpose: §5 — Turning the spec into code: work units (the smallest reproducible SPMC transformation), dual-read task-type definitions, discovery records, and generated rationale traces.

contexts.label: Verification
contexts.purpose: §6 — Deterministic gates with versioned criteria. Each verification enforces a principle/contract/model element and declares its kind; verdicts are the outcomes.

contexts.label: Delivery
contexts.purpose: §7 — Shipping value: features (the smallest independently valuable, verifiable, flow-shaped subgraph of the What) and releases (a coherent, closed cut of features).

contexts.label: Composition
contexts.purpose: The top layer: a product is a composition of archetype instances sharing one domain model; an archetype is a reusable, pre-filled How for a recurring system shape.

contexts.label: Live What-Graph View
contexts.purpose: §3 tooling — a read-only, live-updating visual projection of the What graph that cleanly separates the domain model (§3.1 structure) from the event model (§3.2 behaviour) so a team can view and discuss it while an LLM mutates the graph via MCP.

contexts.label: Shop UI
contexts.purpose: A UI-rich demo slice: the checkout screen and its §3.2.2/§4.5 UI sub-model.

entities.label: Bounded Context
entities.definition: A region within which a term has exactly one meaning (§3.1). The domain model is a set of these, never one flat model.
entities.identity: Identified by name; its ubiquitous language is authoritative within it

entities.label: Entity
entities.definition: A domain concept with identity. May be an aggregate root. Belongs to exactly one bounded context and carries a business-language definition.
entities.identity: Has explicit identity (what makes one instance distinct)

entities.label: Value Object
entities.definition: A domain concept without identity (e.g. Money, Address), defined by its attributes and living in a bounded context.

entities.label: Relation
entities.definition: A typed connection between two entities, carrying cardinality and rationale (why this link and this cardinality).

entities.label: Invariant
entities.definition: A rule that must always hold, stated as a checkable constraint and realised as a machine-checkable SHACL constraint downstream.

entities.label: Context Mapping
entities.definition: An explicit, declared correspondence between concepts in two different bounded contexts (§3.1) — never assumed.

entities.label: Event
entities.definition: A thing that happened — past tense, immutable. Always changes a domain entity (§3.2, the load-bearing relation); behaviour may not reference structure that does not exist.
entities.identity: Distinguished by the fact it records (a past occurrence)

entities.label: Command
entities.definition: An intent that causes events; targets an aggregate (entity) and emits at least one event.

entities.label: Read Model
entities.definition: A view built to look at or decide on; projects at least one entity/event (read-model provenance: no ghost views).

entities.label: Wireframe Step
entities.definition: What the user sees at a step; triggers a command or displays a read model, stated in ubiquitous-language terms.

entities.label: Flow
entities.definition: An ordered behaviour: a timeline of wireframe → command → event(s) → read model.

entities.label: Top Decision
entities.definition: A foundational choice (project shape, layout, layering) that carries rationale and licenses principles (§4.1).

entities.label: Principle
entities.definition: A rule a top decision licenses, stated checkably. Must earn its place by being applied by a work unit or enforced by a verification.

entities.label: Pattern
entities.definition: A concrete shape that realises a principle; what a work unit emits.

entities.label: Application Contract
entities.definition: Invariant code-shaping decisions (language, layering, organization, persistence) that hold across every instance.

entities.label: Infrastructure Contract
entities.definition: Concrete runtime choices for an instance; frozen once chosen; must satisfy (conform to) the application contract.

entities.label: Repository Layout Model
entities.definition: A glob-based declaration of which files are legal where (§4.3). Authored as YAML.

entities.label: Interface Contract
entities.definition: A published interface in an industry-standard format (OpenAPI/AsyncAPI/Protobuf), generated from (derived from) the domain/event model, not hand-written (§4.4).

entities.label: Work Unit
entities.definition: The smallest reproducible unit of realisation (SPMC): one bounded transformation, frozen input, one artifact (§5).

entities.label: Task-Type Definition
entities.definition: A dual-read schema where the dispatch input equals the capture form equals the acceptance criteria. Authored as YAML.

entities.label: Discovery Record
entities.definition: A type-aware capture of an exploratory session, recorded as a diff against the framework layers.

entities.label: Rationale Trace
entities.definition: A generated record linking a produced artifact to the decisions that caused it; must be true (gated by verifications).

entities.label: Verification
entities.definition: A deterministic gate with versioned criteria; enforces a principle/contract/model element and names what it protects (§6). Declares its kind.

entities.label: Verification Kind
entities.definition: The category of a verification (e.g. layout-conformance, internal-coherence, contract-conformance, seam, domain-conformance, behavioural-conformance, cross-archetype), ordered cheapest-first.

entities.label: Verdict
entities.definition: The outcome of a verification (or a composition of verifications, e.g. release_done).

entities.label: Feature
entities.definition: The smallest independently valuable and verifiable slice; a flow-shaped, non-empty subgraph of the What (§7).

entities.label: Release
entities.definition: A chosen, coherent set of features; a subgraph whose cut must be closed (§7).

entities.label: Product
entities.definition: The top: a composition of archetype instances sharing one domain model.

entities.label: Archetype
entities.definition: A reusable, pre-filled How for a recurring system shape. A product realises one or more.

entities.label: How Contract
entities.definition: §4 — the authoring surface for an archetype's complete How: the Why cascade (top decisions → principles → patterns), the application and infrastructure contracts, the repository layout model reference, and the interface contracts. One per archetype.
entities.identity: Identified by its archetype; one How contract per archetype

entities.label: View Graph
entities.definition: The renderable projection of the What graph: a set of View Nodes and View Edges, partitioned into a domain lane and an event lane.
entities.identity: Derived per request from the current set of What-graph source files under .product/

entities.label: View Node
entities.definition: A single renderable node carrying id, label, kind, model lane, and owning context.
entities.identity: node id (mirrors the source What-graph node id)

entities.label: View Edge
entities.definition: A single renderable edge between two View Nodes, carrying its edge kind; a bridge edge crosses the lane divider.

entities.label: Model Lane
entities.definition: A fixed horizontal partition of the canvas: the domain lane (left, §3.1) or the event lane (right, §3.2).

entities.label: Cart
entities.definition: A shopper's basket of line items.

entities.label: System
entities.definition: §3.2.5 — a first-class system: the named thing (kind, purpose, target platforms/classes) a page graph and flows belong to; a What may describe several over one domain.

entities.label: Trigger
entities.definition: §3.2.0 — what initiates a command; its source is exactly one of user, external, or automated. Automation and Translation are automated triggers watching a View.

entities.label: Decider
entities.definition: §3.3 — the executable form of behaviour for an aggregate: decide(state,command)->events|reject and evolve(state,event)->state, its signature derived from the event model.

entities.label: Projector
entities.definition: §3.4 — the executable form of a read model: project(state,event)->state, folding exactly the events the read model projects.

entities.label: Named-Algorithm Primitive
entities.definition: §3.5 — the Polanyi floor: an irreducible computation specified by reference plus an I/O contract plus an oracle, exempt from derivation.

value_objects.label: Model Kind
value_objects.definition: An enumerated tag — domain | event — derived from a node's source NodeKind; it assigns the node to exactly one Model Lane.

relations.label: changes
relations.rationale: The load-bearing relation (§3.2): every event must change exactly one real domain entity — behaviour may not reference structure that does not exist. One entity is changed by many events over time.

relations.label: targets
relations.rationale: A command expresses intent against exactly one aggregate (entity) it operates on; an aggregate is targeted by many commands.

relations.label: emits
relations.rationale: Command coverage (§3.2): a command must produce at least one event; an event is emitted by the command that caused it.

relations.label: projects (entity)
relations.rationale: Read-model provenance (§3.2): a read model must be built from at least one entity (no ghost views); an entity feeds many read models.

relations.label: projects (event)
relations.rationale: A read model may also project the events it is built from; an event can feed many read models.

relations.label: in context
relations.rationale: §3.1: an entity belongs to exactly one bounded context (never a flat model); a context contains many entities.

relations.label: in context
relations.rationale: §3.2: an event lives in exactly one bounded context; a context contains many events.

relations.label: in context
relations.rationale: A command lives in exactly one bounded context, sharing that context's ubiquitous language; a context contains many commands.

relations.label: maps to
relations.rationale: §3.1: a context mapping connects two concepts (entities) in different bounded contexts — an explicit declared correspondence, never assumed.

relations.label: realizes
relations.rationale: §4.1: a pattern is a concrete shape that realises at least one principle; a principle can be realised by several patterns.

relations.label: licenses
relations.rationale: §4.1: a top decision is what makes a principle apply — it licenses zero or more; a principle is licensed by the decision that introduces it.

relations.label: applies (principle)
relations.rationale: §5/§4.1 earn-their-place: a work unit emits code shaped by the principles it applies; a principle applied by a work unit (or enforced by a verification) earns its architectural place.

relations.label: applies (pattern)
relations.rationale: §5: a work unit emits the patterns it applies; a pattern is applied across many work units.

relations.label: enforces (principle)
relations.rationale: §6.1: every verification must name what it protects by enforcing a principle (or contract/model element); a principle can be enforced by several verifications.

relations.label: enforces (contract)
relations.rationale: §6.1: a verification may instead (or also) enforce an application contract — naming the contract it protects.

relations.label: verification kind
relations.rationale: §6.2: a verification declares exactly one kind, which orders it (cheapest gate first); a kind classifies many verifications.

relations.label: yields
relations.rationale: §6: a verification yields a verdict as its outcome; a composite verdict (e.g. release_done) composes several verifications' verdicts.

relations.label: conforms to
relations.rationale: §4.2: an infrastructure contract must satisfy (conform to) the application contract; one application contract is satisfied by many infrastructure contracts.

relations.label: derived from
relations.rationale: §4.4: an interface contract is generated from (derived from) the domain/event model, not hand-written; it draws on many entities.

relations.label: derived from
relations.rationale: §5: a work unit declares its frozen input — the feature (a flow-shaped slice of the What) it is derived from; one feature drives many work units.

relations.label: derived from
relations.rationale: §5: a rationale trace links a produced artifact back to the top decisions that caused it; a decision shows up in many traces.

relations.label: contains
relations.rationale: §7: a feature is a non-empty, flow-shaped subgraph of the What — it contains at least one flow; a flow ships within the feature it belongs to.

relations.label: contains
relations.rationale: §7: a release contains at least one feature and its cut must be closed (dependencies included); a feature ships in one release at a time.

relations.label: realises
relations.rationale: Composition: a product realises one or more archetypes (their pre-filled How); a reusable archetype is realised across many products.

relations.label: realised by
relations.rationale: Composition: a bounded context is realised by an archetype instance; one archetype can realise several contexts of the same shape.

relations.label: shares domain model
relations.rationale: Composition: a product is a set of archetype instances sharing one domain model — it contains the bounded contexts of that single shared model.

relations.label: in context
relations.rationale: §3.1: like an entity, a value object belongs to exactly one bounded context, though it has no identity of its own; a context contains many value objects.

relations.label: described by
relations.rationale: §3.1: an entity is described by value objects (e.g. an attribute typed as Money or Address); a value-object type is reused across many entities.

relations.label: connects
relations.rationale: §3.1: a relation is a typed connection naming a source and a target entity; an entity participates in many relations.

relations.label: constrains
relations.rationale: §3.1: an invariant constrains the entity (aggregate) whose rule it states; an entity may carry many invariants.

relations.label: triggers
relations.rationale: §3.2: a wireframe step issues (triggers) the command behind a user action; a command may be triggered from several steps.

relations.label: displays
relations.rationale: §3.2: a wireframe step displays a read model to the user; a read model may surface in several steps.

relations.label: derived from
relations.rationale: §4.3: the repository layout model declares which files are legal where; it is derived from the project-shape/layout top decision that licenses it.

relations.label: enforces (layout)
relations.rationale: §6.2: the layout-conformance verification (the cheapest gate, run first) enforces the repository layout model — naming what it protects.

relations.label: typed by
relations.rationale: §5: a work unit is dispatched and accepted per a task-type definition (dual-read: dispatch input = capture form = acceptance criteria); one task type governs many work units.

relations.label: informs
relations.rationale: §5: a discovery record captures an exploratory session as a diff against the framework layers; its findings inform top decisions and other layers.

relations.label: is How of
relations.rationale: §4: a How contract is the authoring surface for exactly one archetype's How; an archetype has exactly one current How contract.

relations.label: bundles
relations.rationale: §4.1: a How contract bundles the archetype's Why cascade — the top decisions (and the principles/patterns they license/realise) that found the How.

relations.label: carries
relations.rationale: §4.2: a How contract carries exactly one application contract (required) — the invariant code-shaping decisions stable across the archetype's instances.

relations.label: references
relations.rationale: §4.3: a How contract references the archetype's repository layout model (which files are legal where).

relations.label: lists
relations.rationale: §4.4: a How contract lists the archetype's published interface contracts, each generated from the domain model.

relations.label: node in lane
relations.rationale: Every View Node belongs to exactly one Model Lane, fixed by its Model Kind.

relations.label: issues
relations.rationale: a Trigger initiates exactly one Command (§3.2.0)

relations.label: owns
relations.rationale: a flow belongs to exactly one system (§3.2.5)

relations.label: decides for
relations.rationale: a Decider governs one aggregate entity (§3.3)

relations.label: projects for
relations.rationale: a Projector builds one read model (§3.4)

invariants.statement: §3.1 An entity must carry a business-language definition.
§3.1 An entity must belong to exactly one bounded context (never a flat model).
§3.1 A relation must declare a cardinality.
§3.1 A relation must carry rationale (why this link and this cardinality).
§3.1 A context mapping must connect two concepts (an explicit declared correspondence, never assumed).
§3.1 A context mapping must state the correspondence (e.g. linked by PersonId).
§3.1 An invariant must be stated as a checkable constraint.
§3.2 Every event must change a real domain entity (the load-bearing relation; behaviour may not reference structure that does not exist).
§3.2 An event must live in a bounded context.
§3.2 A command must target a real aggregate (entity).
§3.2 A command must emit at least one event (command coverage).
§3.2 A read model must project at least one entity/event (read-model provenance: no ghost views).
§4.1 A top decision must carry rationale (what it buys, when it applies).
§4.1 A principle must be stated checkably (a vague principle cannot back a verification).
§4.1 Earn-their-place: a principle must be applied by a work unit or enforced by a verification, else it is documentation, not architecture.
§4.1 A pattern must realise at least one principle.
§4.2 An infrastructure contract must satisfy (conform to) the application contract.
§4.4 An interface contract must be generated from (derived from) the domain/event model, not hand-written.
§5 A work unit must declare its (frozen) input — what it is derived from.
§6.1 Every verification must name what it protects (enforces a principle/contract/model element).
§6.2 A verification must declare its kind.
§7 A feature must be a non-empty subgraph of the What (flow-shaped, not a free-floating ticket).
§7 A release must contain at least one feature; its cut must be closed (checked at validation time against dependencies).
§5/§4.1 The trace must be true (the crown rule): every principle a work unit applies must be enforced by a passing verification, or the claim must be retracted.
§4.2 An application contract must be named.
§4.2 Every application-contract statement must be checkable (precise enough for a verification to confirm) — a convention that cannot be checked cannot be a contract.
§4.2 An infrastructure contract must be frozen once chosen (frozen = true), selected per instance at Discovery.
§4 A How contract must declare the archetype it belongs to and carry an application contract.

A View Node's lane is derived from its source NodeKind: structural kinds (Entity, ValueObject, Relation, Invariant, ContextMapping) map to the domain lane; behavioural kinds (Event, Command, ReadModel, WireframeStep, Flow) map to the event lane.
Every bridge edge runs from a node in the event lane to a node in the domain lane (behaviour acts on structure); the domain lane never references the event lane.
A system's interaction classes must be recognised (gui or tui) — the gating context dimension (§3.2.2).
A trigger's source is exactly one of user, external, or automated; an automated trigger must watch a View (§3.2.0).
A Decider must have at least one reachable rejection — it must enforce an invariant, or it should not exist (§3.3 Decider justification).

context_mappings.rationale: A feature (Delivery) is a flow-shaped subgraph of the What (the Model) — Delivery does not copy model nodes, it shares the very same Flow/Entity nodes. The Model and Delivery contexts share a kernel.

Verification enforces principles authored in The Why; the principle's checkable statement is the contract between them. The Why is the supplier of statements, Verification the customer that proves they hold.
Work units (Realisation) emit code that must conform to the Contracts context's application contract; Realisation is a conformist downstream of Contracts, taking the contract as given.

wireframe_steps.label: Facilitator captures artifacts; reviews open questions
Run finalize to export conformant Turtle + provenance

flows.label: Model with the event model, view both

reification_rules.rationale: lists render as ItemList on mobile
quantity uses a NumberStepper on mobile

systems.label: Bookstore Demo
systems.purpose: the seeded bookstore What model used for demos and workshops

systems.label: Product CLI
systems.purpose: the product binary: author and verify a What/How framework graph from the terminal

systems.label: Product View
systems.purpose: the live Event-Modeling web view served over the MCP HTTP server
